text: The video shows the ovary completely adhered due to previous surgery, with visible damage and areas of endometriosis. The deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIA) is seen invading the ovary and the rectosigmoid colon. The interface between the lesion and normal tissue is obscured by fibrosis, complicating the identification of clear resection margins.

The video shows the ovary tube attached with visible lesions on its surface. The lesions appear abnormal, with the peritoneal side displaying deeply fibrotic tissue. This fibrotic tissue complicates the identification of clear margins for resection.

The surgeon navigates to the paracervical space on the left side, encountering fibrotic peritoneal tissue. In the caudal region, a deep lesion is present, but its margins are indistinct. The rectovaginal space is accessed, revealing the rectum attached to thickened peritoneum, suggesting extensive endometriosis. The challenge of identifying clear margins for resection is evident due to the fibrotic tissue and indistinct lesion boundaries.

The surgeon identifies a deep lesion penetrating the rectosigmoid colon. The pararectal space on the left side is open, and the rectovaginal space has been entered, allowing access to tissue from the posterior cervix.

The surgeon observes a lesion on the anterior rectum, noting the difficulty in assessing its size and extent. During laparoscopic surgery, deep lesions are being removed from the posterior cul-de-sac.
The surgical team identifies and resects a lesion on the right side, carefully locating the ureter and assessing the lesion's depth near the peritoneum before excising it.
The surgeon removes a suspension suture from the ovary, revealing a deep lesion within the ovary that is excised. The other ovary appears undamaged and healthy.
